In addition to molecular formula, we are also interested in knowing the molecular structure (i.e. *constitutional formula*) of the compound – the structural arrangement by which the atoms have bonded to form the compound.

Figure 3.6a shows the molecular structure of a glucose molecule. A single line symbolizes a single covalent bond between atoms and a double line symbolizes a double covalent bond. Similarly, if there were a triple covalent bond between atoms present in this molecule, it would be symbolized by a triple line. In this example, the structure of glucose is shown in an open chain format which appears to exist as a two-dimensional structure. However, it is important to note that in reality, molecules and compounds exist as three-dimensional structures. Furthermore, many compounds are also formed in special structural shapes. For example, the majority of glucose molecules in our bodies are stored in a ring-like structure comprising a ring-shaped 'carbon skeleton' with hydroxyl groups attached (hydroxyl groups consist of an oxygen and carbon atom joined by a single covalent bond) (see Figure 3.6b). Given that organic molecules and compounds are often relatively big, we sometimes draw their constitutional formula using shorthand methods (see Figure 3.6c).

## Definition

The molecular structure (constitutional formula) of a compound is the structural arrangement by which the atoms have bonded to form the compound. It complements the molecular formula by specifying how atoms are connected, not just which atoms are present.

## Core Principles

- A single line symbolizes a single covalent bond between atoms; a double line symbolizes a double covalent bond; a triple line symbolizes a triple covalent bond.
- Although constitutional formulae are typically drawn in two dimensions (e.g. an open-chain depiction), molecules and compounds in reality exist as three-dimensional structures.
- Many compounds adopt special structural shapes (e.g. ring-like structures with a 'carbon skeleton').
- Functional groups such as hydroxyl groups (an oxygen and carbon atom joined by a single covalent bond) can be attached to a carbon skeleton.

## Sub-concepts

- Open-chain structure (e.g. glucose drawn as an open chain, Figure 3.6a).
- Ring-like structure (e.g. the majority of glucose molecules in our bodies are stored in a ring-shaped carbon skeleton with hydroxyl groups attached, Figure 3.6b).
- Hydroxyl group: oxygen and carbon atom joined by a single covalent bond.
- Shorthand notation for constitutional formulae of large organic molecules (Figure 3.6c).

## Practical Applications

- Drawing constitutional formulae of organic molecules and compounds, often using shorthand methods because organic molecules are relatively large (Figure 3.6c).
- Representing biologically relevant molecules such as glucose in either open-chain or ring form depending on context (e.g. storage form in the body is predominantly ring-shaped).
